local_names.romanized_local: Nandu Masala (Tamil); Kekda Masala (Hindi); Njandu Masala (Malayalam); Peethala Iguru (Telugu)

region_of_origin: South India (Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Andhra Pradesh), India

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Crab Masala as a prepared South Indian dish; mapped to closest crab-based food product class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for spiced cooked crab preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Crab Masala; A0CJW (cooked crustaceans) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Crab Masala as a prepared dish; left null to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Crab Masala is approximately 200 g, typically comprising one or two pieces of shell-on mud crab coated in a thick, semi-dry spiced masala, served alongside steamed rice or rasam rice.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (NIN), Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for cooked crab and spiced seafood preparations. Values for the full masala preparation (including spice paste, onion, tomato, and oil) are estimated by combining crab meat composition with typical masala ingredient contributions. USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, crab cooked, moist heat, FDC ID 175180) was used as a cross-reference for core macros and micronutrients. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and cholesterol estimated from USDA SR Legacy crab data adjusted for oil contribution.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible net digestible carbohydrates; University of Sydney GI Database consulted, no entry found for crab masala or equivalent shellfish preparation
gi_source.source_match_notes: Crab Masala is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with only 4.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, of which a significant portion is dietary fibre and spice-derived complex carbohydrates. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a GI value for crab masala or equivalent shellfish masala preparations. Given the negligible net digestible carbohydrate content, glycemic index is not applicable. gi_category is set to 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving is effectively 0 and set to 0.0. The numeric field glycemic_index is set to 0 as a sentinel consistent with not_applicable gi_category, not as a measured GI value.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (380 mg per 100 g) from shellfish and added salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and avoid additional salt, as crab naturally contains sodium and the masala preparation adds further sodium; the potassium content (215 mg per 100 g) partially offsets the sodium load and supports blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low net digestible carbohydrate content and high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Crab Masala is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycemic impact, high protein content (12.8 g per 100 g) supporting satiety and glucose stability, and low sugar content (0.8 g per 100 g); portion control of accompanying rice is recommended to manage overall glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids, selenium, and low saturated fat profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Crab Masala provides cardioprotective omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and selenium (32.5 mcg per 100 g), with relatively low saturated fat (approximately 0.9 g per 100 g); however, the moderate sodium content (380 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for individuals with heart failure or sodium-restricted cardiovascular diets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids and selenium
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The omega-3 fatty acids and selenium in crab have documented anti-inflammatory properties beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis; however, individuals with comorbid gout should limit intake due to moderate purine content in shellfish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crab Masala, known as Nandu Masala in Tamil Nadu and Njandu Masala in Kerala, is a fiery and intensely flavoured coastal South Indian seafood preparation. It is made with fresh mud crabs that are cracked open and slow-sautéed in a concentrated spice paste comprising black pepper, fennel seeds, red chilli, turmeric, coriander, ginger, garlic, curry leaves, onion, and tomato, cooked until the moisture reduces to a thick, semi-dry coating that clings to the shells. The cooking process allows the aromatic spices to penetrate the crab shells and infuse the sweet, delicate crab meat with a complex, roasted spice flavour. The dish is characterised by a bold, peppery heat balanced by the anise-like sweetness of fennel and the tanginess of the tomato-onion base. Texturally, the crab meat is tender and juicy while the masala coating is thick and intensely flavoured. Nutritionally, Crab Masala is a high-protein (12.8 g per 100 g), moderate-fat (5.4 g per 100 g) dish with very low carbohydrate content (4.2 g per 100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and high-protein dietary patterns. It is an excellent source of vitamin B12 (4.2 mcg per 100 g), selenium (32.5 mcg per 100 g), zinc (2.8 mg per 100 g), and phosphorus (145 mg per 100 g). The dish has negligible glycemic impact and is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content (380 mg per 100 g) requires attention for hypertensive individuals. It is a prized dish in coastal Indian cuisine, typically served as a special side dish with steamed rice or rasam rice during lunch or dinner, and is particularly popular during the monsoon season when mud crabs are at their best.
common_user_queries: Is Crab Masala good for diabetes?
Is Crab Masala high in protein?
What are the calories in Crab Masala?
What spices are used in Crab Masala?
Can I eat Crab Masala if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked crab, moist heat). No single approved source provides exact per-100g values for the full Crab Masala preparation including spice paste and oil; values represent a defensible estimate for the composite dish. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: water_g set to -1: moisture content for the full Crab Masala preparation (including spice paste reduction) is not directly reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for this composite dish; no approved source provides a moisture value for this specific preparation, hence -1 is used per the negative sentinel value policy. Saturated fat (0.9 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.8 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.6 g), and cholesterol (55 mg) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked crab (FDC ID 175180) adjusted for oil contribution from the masala preparation. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible for this preparation type per USDA SR Legacy. Glycemic index field is set to numeric 0 as a sentinel value consistent with gi_category 'not_applicable'; this is not a measured GI value. Crab Masala is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate shellfish dish; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible net digestible carbohydrates. The University of Sydney GI Database was consulted and contains no entry for crab masala or equivalent shellfish masala preparation. The input dietary_tags incorrectly listed 'vegetarian' and 'vegan' — corrected to non-vegetarian and non-vegan as the dish contains crab (shellfish). Duplicate portions in input (four identical entries) — deduplicated to a single 200 g serving. Aliases consistency self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language (english: Crab Masala, Mud Crab Masala, Coastal Crab Curry, South Indian Crab Masala; tamil: நண்டு மசாலா, கைகட்டு மசாலா; hindi: कैकड़ा मसाला; malayalam: ഞണ്ട് മസാല; telugu: పీతల ఇగురు; romanized: Nandu Masala, Nandu Kuzhambu, Kekda Masala, Njandu Masala, Peethala Iguru) verified present verbatim in flat aliases array.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Crab Masala', a precise canonical English name. Tamil (நண்டு மசாலா) and Hindi (कैकड़ा मसाला) local names present. Malayalam and Telugu names included in aliases_by_language and flat aliases. Cuisine is Coastal South Indian — G6 does not mandate Chinese/Malay/Tamil/Hindi for Indian dishes (only Singaporean dishes require that set); Indian dish multilingual coverage is satisfied by regional language aliases.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (115.0), protein_g (12.8), fat_g (5.4), carbohydrate_g (4.2) all present and numeric. Source: ICMR-NIN cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. nutrient_source fully populated.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (1.2), sodium_mg (380.0), saturated_fat_g (0.9), sugar_g (0.8) all present as numeric values. Values are plausible for a spiced crab preparation.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to numeric 0 as a sentinel consistent with gi_category 'not_applicable'. gi_category is 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. gi_source fully documented with University of Sydney GI Database consultation noted and source_match_notes explaining the sentinel usage. The field is numeric as required by the validator.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI mapped to crab-based food product class (closest_parent match documented). FoodEx2 code A0CJW (cooked crustaceans) used as closest_parent defensible anchor with gap note. SNOMED CT left null with honest gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (approximately 1,450 characters). Covers: what the food is, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries and ingredient_synonyms populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate, sodium/potassium), Type 2 Diabetes (low, low carb/high protein), Cardiovascular Disease (low, omega-3/selenium/sodium). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly with machine key type2_diabetes.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: Flat aliases array contains 14 entries covering English variants, Tamil, Hindi, Malayalam, Telugu scripts, and romanized forms. aliases_by_language populated for english, tamil, hindi, malayalam, telugu, and romanized. Self-check performed: every string value in aliases_by_language verified present verbatim in flat aliases array. No discrepancies found.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types to 'closest_parent'; documented water_g = -1 in notes.

Iteration 3: Fixed glycemic_index from null to numeric 0 to satisfy validator requirement that the field be numeric; expanded gi_source notes to clarify sentinel usage. Expanded notes to fully document water_g = -1 basis per g3 negative sentinel rule, citing ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central as consulted sources confirming unavailability. Verified all aliases_by_language strings present verbatim in flat aliases array to satisfy q8.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: EX000020 Crab Masala passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. Key fixes in iteration 3: glycemic_index corrected to numeric 0 (sentinel for not_applicable) to satisfy g3 numeric hygiene and q4 glycemic completeness; water_g = -1 fully documented in notes with source consultation basis; aliases consistency verified with all aliases_by_language strings confirmed present in flat aliases array.
